subject: Chemistry
title: Prussian Blue: The First Modern Pigment
description: A quick win: understand Prussian Blue: The First Modern Pigment.

hook.text: Prussian blue was discovered by accident in 1706 when a paint maker used contaminated potash. It became the first modern pigment.

buildup.text: Heinrich Diesbach was making red pigment but his potash was tainted with animal blood. The result was deep blue.

discovery.text: The color comes from iron atoms in two oxidation states: Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺. Electrons jump between them, absorbing red light.

twist.text: Prussian blue is also a medical antidote. It traps radioactive cesium and thallium in the gut, preventing absorption.

climax.text: Blueprints were made with Prussian blue chemistry. The word 'blueprint' exists because of a paint accident.

punchline.text: Blood-contaminated potash, iron electrons jumping. An accident that colored the world.

quiz.question: What causes Prussian blue's intense color?
options: Electrons jumping between iron atoms in two different oxidation states
Cobalt molecules reflecting only blue wavelengths
